topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor had been overly cheerful, practically beaming as she pointed out the "unique character" of the property. I just stared at the faded photographs she’d brought, my jaw tight. Every corner of this house felt wrong, tainted. I traced a finger over a scuff mark on the hardwood floor in the living room, a deliberate, slow movement. It wasn't just wear; it was something deeper, something sticky that refused to come off. I remembered the old neighborhood gossip, the whispers about the unsolved case from twenty years ago. Now, standing here, the silence of the rooms amplified every creak, every shadow. I moved to the basement, pulling back the dusty drop cloth. Beneath it, the concrete floor was stained, dark patches that looked suspiciously like dried residue. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a sharp, unpleasant feeling that demanded retribution. This place had held secrets, and now I was the keeper of the knowledge, the one who could finally expose the rot.

[story 2]

"It's charming, isn't it?" the agent chirped, completely oblivious to the tension radiating off me. I managed a tight, brittle smile that didn't reach my eyes. Charming. I thought of the forensics reports I’d skimmed—the meticulous cataloging of evidence that had once saturated these walls. I walked into the master bedroom, running my hand along the baseboard, feeling the slight grit beneath my fingertips. It felt like a physical accusation. I kicked a loose floorboard near the closet, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet space. I needed to see the evidence, to confirm what I already suspected. I found a loose section of plaster near the fireplace mantel. With a sudden, forceful heave, I pried it up. What I saw wasn't just crumbling plaster; it was discoloration, patterns that spoke of violence and hasty cleanup. A slow, burning resentment started to bubble up, hot and consuming, pushing me toward the door.

[story 3]

I paced the length of the dining room, my boots clicking aggressively on the polished wood. Every piece of furniture seemed to hold a memory, and mine was a heavy weight of unwelcome intrusion. I stopped by the kitchen counter, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge. The faint, metallic scent lingered in the air, almost imperceptible, but potent enough to make my throat constrict. I deliberately poured a glass of water, the sound of the liquid hitting the glass sounding unnaturally loud. I leaned in close to the window overlooking the overgrown backyard, my gaze fixed on the disturbed earth near the old rose bushes. It was the aftermath, the sloppy erasure of something terrible. A low, guttural sound escaped my chest—a sound of pure, simmering dissatisfaction. This house hadn't just been empty; it had been used, violated, and then tried to be forgotten. I wanted to tear down every layer of that forgotten history, to make sure no one could ever pretend this was just a quiet suburban home.
